docs.title: ProtocolWarden
docs.text:
ProtocolWarden is a contract-first AI operations ecosystem organized around semantic repo graphs, execution protocols, policy-controlled routing, runtime adapters, and public-safe architecture projection.

This site is the canonical public knowledge surface for the ecosystem. It explains what exists, how it fits together, what boundaries are enforced, and what is public versus private.

The short front door is the GitHub profile README. The GitHub org lists all public repos.

docs.title: This Site Is
docs.text:
• the public documentation surface
  • the architecture explanation layer
  • the protocol and specification hub
  • the ontology and topology explorer
  • the governance and boundary reference

docs.title: This Site Is Not
docs.text:
• a runtime system
  • an orchestration layer
  • a deployment manager
  • a backend service
  • an execution environment

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:

  • GitHub org
  • Getting Started

docs.title: Ecosystem in 60 Seconds
docs.text:
• CxRP owns cross-repo execution and routing semantics
  • RxP owns runtime invocation semantics
  • OperationsCenter owns orchestration and governance behavior
  • SwitchBoard owns lane and backend selection
  • ExecutorRuntime owns runtime invocation mechanics
  • RepoGraph owns the shared ontology, topology, projection, and boundary language
  • PlatformManifest publishes the public graph instance and public-safe projections
  • the private-truth layer supplies private graph truth in that language
  • Custodian consumes RepoGraph boundary artifacts and enforces public-surface drift checks
  • PlatformDeployment owns deployment and local hosting concerns
  • adjacent backend consumers are runtime and content-pipeline consumers

For a short operator model, see architecture/simple-platform-model.md.

docs.title: Protocol Stack Summary
docs.text:
• Contracts: CxRP, RxP
  • Control plane: OperatorConsole, OperationsCenter, SwitchBoard
  • Runtime layer: ExecutorRuntime, adjacent backend consumers, managed backends
  • Inventory and governance: PlatformManifest, Custodian, SourceRegistry, PlatformDeployment
  • Utility tooling: Warehouse

docs.title: Operational References
docs.text:
• RepoGraph Post-PASS Hardening Baseline
  • Semantic Federation

docs.title: Documentation Philosophy
docs.text: This repository exists to preserve architectural intent and reduce cognitive load. The public site makes the ecosystem understandable without forcing contributors to reverse-engineer the platform from source. For the short front door, see the separate ProtocolWarden repository.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
This section explains how the ProtocolWarden ecosystem is split across documentation, manifest language, private graph truth, deployment topography, governance, control, runtime, and utility planes.

Start with the Platform Architecture Charter.

docs.title: Core Pages
docs.text:
• Platform Architecture Charter
  • Simple Platform Model
  • Layered Architecture
  • Orchestration Model
  • Execution Flow
  • Runtime Binding
  • Capability Model
  • Artifact Flow
  • Audit Architecture
  • Observability Model
  • Topology Model

docs.title: Stable Baselines
docs.text: • RepoGraph Post-PASS Hardening

docs.title: Artifact Flow
docs.text: Artifacts move across the system as evidence, outputs, and references rather than as ad hoc undocumented byproducts.

docs.title: Typical flow
docs.text:
1. execution produces artifacts
  2. runtime and orchestration layers normalize references
  3. observability systems retain evidence
  4. governance layers validate exposure and drift
  5. public documentation surfaces may expose only sanitized metadata

docs.title: Audit Architecture
docs.text: Auditing is not a sidecar concern. It is a core reliability and governance mechanism.

docs.title: Audit roles
docs.text:
• verify invariant compliance
  • detect drift
  • catch privacy leakage
  • preserve architectural doctrine
  • support change review

docs.title: Main enforcement surfaces
docs.text:
• schema validation
  • projection validation
  • representative verification suites
  • Custodian detector runs

docs.title: Capability Model
docs.text: Capabilities describe what lanes, runtimes, or managed projects can safely and meaningfully do.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• execution mode support
  • runtime kind support
  • validation behavior
  • artifact production support
  • environment requirements

Capability governance should remain explicit and testable rather than implicit.

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant OC as OperatorConsole
    participant OPS as OperationsCenter
    participant PM as PlatformManifest
    participant SB as SwitchBoard
    participant ER as ExecutorRuntime
    participant MP as ManagedProject
    participant CU as Custodian

    OC->>OPS: task intent
    OPS->>PM: resolve topology and visibility
    OPS->>SB: route proposal using CxRP
    SB-->>OPS: lane decision
    OPS->>ER: invoke runtime using RxP
    ER->>MP: execute managed project workflow
    MP-->>ER: artifacts and reports
    ER-->>OPS: normalized runtime result
    OPS->>CU: validate policy and hygiene boundaries

docs.title: Layered Architecture
docs.text:
The ecosystem is easiest to understand as layered responsibilities.

graph TD
    DOCS[Public Documentation Layer\nProtocolWarden.github.io]
    GOV[Governance and Inventory Layer\nPlatformManifest / private topology repos / Custodian / SourceRegistry]
    PROTO[Protocol Layer\nCxRP / RxP]
    CTRL[Control Plane\nOperatorConsole / OperationsCenter / SwitchBoard]
    RUN[Runtime and Managed Projects\nExecutorRuntime / managed projects / backends]
    HOST[Deployment and Hosting\nPlatformDeployment]

    DOCS --> GOV
    GOV --> PROTO
    PROTO --> CTRL
    CTRL --> RUN
    HOST --> CTRL
    HOST --> RUN

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• documentation explains the ecosystem
  • governance and inventory describe and constrain it
  • protocol repos define canonical semantics
  • control-plane repos make decisions
  • runtime systems execute work
  • hosting systems run the local environment

docs.title: Observability Model
docs.text:
Observability in this ecosystem is about more than logs.

It includes:

  • artifacts
  • traces
  • policy findings
  • representative runs
  • retained execution records
  • drift reports

The goal is to make behavior explainable after the fact.

docs.title: Orchestration Model
docs.text: OperationsCenter is the orchestration core. It consumes topology, protocol, and governance inputs, but it does not own all of those semantics.

docs.title: Main orchestration responsibilities
docs.text:
• planning
  • policy evaluation
  • routing requests
  • execution coordination
  • evidence retention

docs.title: Non-responsibilities
docs.text:
• owning CxRP wire semantics
  • owning RxP runtime invocation semantics
  • owning platform topology truth
  • owning deployment lifecycle

docs.title: Platform Architecture Charter
docs.text:
ProtocolWarden.github.io is the canonical public architecture charter for the ProtocolWarden ecosystem. It explains what planes exist, what each plane owns, what each plane must not own, and how the public, private, and local manifest layers fit together.

The short public front door lives in the separate ProtocolWarden repository. This charter is the longer public knowledge surface.

docs.title: Charter vs Manifest
docs.text:
The charter is doctrine. It states what must be true.

The manifest repositories and enforcement tools implement that doctrine:

ProtocolWarden.github.io   = architecture charter, doctrine, public explanation
RepoGraph                  = shared graph semantics library
PlatformManifest           = public graph instance / public projection publisher
private-truth layer            = private graph instance / private superset truth
PlatformDeployment         = local/private deployment topography
Custodian                  = boundary artifact consumer and drift enforcement
OperationsCenter           = runtime consumer of composed graph truth

The charter is not the execution engine and it is not the schema authority. The public repo catalog is a governance-controlled projection and is not a place to browse private-truth repos as first-class catalog entries.

docs.title: Ontology
docs.text:
Ontology answers:

What exists?
What does it mean?

Examples:

  • repo kinds
  • system kinds
  • platform plane names
  • owner kinds
  • visibility classes
  • capability categories

docs.title: Topology
docs.text:
Topology answers:

How is it connected?

Topology edges are broader than imports or API calls. Valid platform topology edges include:

  • consumes manifest
  • publishes projection
  • reads artifact
  • writes artifact
  • dispatches request
  • polls status
  • indexes output
  • validates output
  • owns schema
  • implements adapter
  • invokes runtime
  • deploys
  • hosts

docs.title: Topography
docs.text:
Topography answers:

Where and how is the platform deployed in a concrete environment?

Examples:

  • local paths
  • compose files
  • ports
  • env overlays
  • host-specific runtime placement
  • cache roots
  • machine-local endpoints

Topography belongs to PlatformDeployment, not PlatformManifest. RepoGraph may carry only the shared topography definitions that deployment consumers choose to adopt later.

docs.title: Projection
docs.text:
Projection answers:

How does private truth become public-safe truth?

Examples:

  • hide private repo names
  • rename private entities
  • redact local paths
  • drop unsafe edge kinds
  • expose only public-safe topology views

docs.title: Ownership Table
docs.text: Repo Owns Must Not Own ProtocolWarden.github.io public charter, public diagrams, doctrine executable manifest semantics, private topology data, local deployment state RepoGraph shared ontology, topology, projection vocabulary, validation primitives, boundary artifact semantics orchestration behavior, private graph truth, deployment overlays PlatformManifest public graph instance, loaders, validators, public-safe projection publication canonical graph semantics, private topology data, local topography, orchestration behavior, CxRP/RxP semantics private-truth layer private graph data, private repo identities, visibility metadata, boundary artifact generation canonical ontology semantics, canonical projection algorithm semantics, local deployment state PlatformDeployment (deployment overlay repo) local/private topography, runtime placement, env overlays, ports, compose layout ontology, public topology language, private topology truth, orchestration policy Custodian audit rules, drift checks, projection safety checks, boundary validation, reports topology truth, projection truth, orchestration behavior, deployment state OperationsCenter orchestration behavior, task binding, dispatch, runtime consumption of composed manifest truth canonical ontology, private manifest data, projection policy definitions, local deployment topology Warehouse code-context packaging utility behavior, kits, crates, pallets, yard mechanics graph authority, topology truth, registry truth, orchestration, governance

docs.title: Public, Private, and Local Layering
docs.text:
RepoGraph
    = shared graph semantics

PlatformManifest
    = public graph instance and public-safe publisher

private-truth layer
    = private graph superset expressed with RepoGraph semantics

Project / WorkScope manifests
    = scoped overlays for project and work assembly

PlatformDeployment local manifests
    = machine/runtime topography overlays only

Composition order:

platform -> private -> (project or work_scope) -> local

docs.title: Warehouse Downgrade Note
docs.text:
Warehouse is intentionally not part of the platform authority boundary.

It is a context-packaging utility for code selection, chunking, staging, and debugging workflows. It must not be modeled as:

  • topology owner
  • registry owner
  • scheduler
  • governance authority
  • runtime artifact dependency for managed projects

docs.title: Dependency Direction
docs.text:
ProtocolWarden.github.io documents everything

RepoGraph defines the language
PlatformManifest and private-truth layer import that language
PlatformDeployment uses composed output for local deployment/topography
OperationsCenter consumes composed truth for orchestration
Custodian consumes RepoGraph-derived boundary artifacts and verifies drift

Avoid circular authority:

  • private-truth layer and other private-truth repositories must not define platform language
  • PlatformDeployment must not define platform language
  • OperationsCenter must not define platform language
  • Warehouse must not define platform language
  • Custodian must not define platform truth

docs.title: Enforcement Model
docs.text:
The charter states what must be true.
RepoGraph encodes the shared graph language.
PlatformManifest publishes the public graph instance.
private-truth layer declares private truth in that language and derives boundary artifacts.
PlatformDeployment declares local deployment/topography overlays.
Custodian verifies that declarations, generated projections, and boundary artifacts do not drift from the rules.
OperationsCenter consumes composed manifest truth at runtime.

docs.title: Platform Planes
docs.text:
graph TD
    DOC[ProtocolWarden.github.io\nDocumentation + Charter]
    RG[RepoGraph\nOntology + Topology + Projection Semantics]
    PM[PlatformManifest\nPublic Graph Instance]
    PRM[private-truth layer\nPrivate Graph Data]
    PD[PlatformDeployment\nTopography + Local Deployment]
    CUST[Custodian\nAudit + Drift Enforcement]
    OC[OperationsCenter\nRuntime Consumer]
    WH[Warehouse\nContext Packaging Utility]

    DOC --> RG
    DOC --> PM
    DOC --> PRM
    DOC --> PD
    RG --> PM
    RG --> PRM
    RG --> PD
    PM --> OC
    PRM --> OC
    PD --> OC
    RG --> CUST
    PRM --> CUST
    PD --> CUST
    WH -. tooling only .-> OC

docs.title: Manifest Layering
docs.text:
graph TD
    Semantics[RepoGraph\nShared semantics]
    Platform[PlatformManifest\nPublic graph instance]
    Private[private-truth layer\nPrivate graph superset]
    Project[Project / WorkScope\nScoped overlays]
    Local[PlatformDeployment LocalManifest\nTopography overlay]
    Effective[EffectiveRepoGraph\nConsumed by OperationsCenter]

    Semantics --> Platform
    Semantics --> Private
    Platform --> Private
    Private --> Project
    Project --> Local
    Local --> Effective

docs.title: Projection Flow
docs.text:
graph LR
    PrivateTruth[private-truth layer truth]
    ProjectionRules[RepoGraph projection rules]
    PublicSafe[PlatformManifest public projection]
    Boundary[RepoGraph boundary artifact]
    Custodian[Custodian validation]

    PrivateTruth --> ProjectionRules
    ProjectionRules --> PublicSafe
    ProjectionRules --> Boundary
    PublicSafe --> Custodian
    Boundary --> Custodian

docs.title: Runtime Binding
docs.text: Runtime binding is the controlled association between an orchestration decision and the runtime mechanics used to execute it.

docs.title: Why it matters
docs.text:
• it separates planning from execution mechanics
  • it supports capability and policy checks
  • it keeps runtime choice explicit and auditable

docs.title: Ownership
docs.text:
• canonical routing and execution semantics: CxRP
  • canonical runtime invocation semantics: RxP
  • runtime binding use and enforcement: OperationsCenter

docs.title: Simple Platform Model
docs.text: RepoGraph defines the language. private-truth layer stores private truth. PlatformManifest publishes safe public views. Custodian verifies boundaries. OperationsCenter orchestrates. PlatformDeployment deploys. Warehouse packages context. ProtocolWarden/ProtocolWarden is the short public front door. ProtocolWarden.github.io is the full public knowledge surface.

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
graph LR
    RG[RepoGraph] --> PM[PlatformManifest]
    RG --> PRM[private-truth layer]
    PRM --> CUST[Custodian]
    PM --> OC[OperationsCenter]
    PD[PlatformDeployment] --> OC
    WH[Warehouse] -. context only .-> OC

docs.title: Boundary artifact flow
docs.text:
graph LR
    PRM[private-truth layer] --> ART[Boundary artifact file]
    ART --> CUST[Custodian]
    CUST --> REP[Verification report]

docs.title: Projection profile flow
docs.text:
graph LR
    PRM[private-truth layer graph truth] --> RG[RepoGraph projection profile]
    RG --> PUB[Public-safe projection]
    RG --> AUD[Audit/internal projection]

The simple model is descriptive only. The RepoGraph schema, projection, and boundary rules still enforce the actual boundaries.

docs.title: Topology Model
docs.text:
Topology answers:

  • what repos exist
  • how they relate
  • which edges are public-safe
  • which systems are control-plane, runtime, or support surfaces

Topology truth belongs in manifest systems, not in ad hoc docs alone. This site explains that topology publicly.

docs.title: RepoGraph Post-PASS Hardening Baseline
docs.text: This page records the known-good architectural checkpoint reached after the RepoGraph migration, hardening, stability, and semantic-federation passes.

docs.title: Baseline guarantees
docs.text:
• RepoGraph remains the canonical graph language and semantic governance owner.
  • private-truth layer remains the owner of private graph truth and boundary artifact generation.
  • PlatformManifest remains the public graph projection publisher.
  • Custodian remains fail-closed and consumes boundary artifacts only.
  • OperationsCenter remains an orchestration consumer of composed graph truth.
  • PlatformDeployment remains deployment/topography overlay only.
  • Warehouse remains a context packaging utility only.

docs.title: Enforced invariants
docs.text:
• REPOGRAPH_BOUNDARY_ARTIFACT_FILE is required for Custodian enforcement.
  • Missing or malformed boundary artifacts fail closed.
  • Projection profiles remain explicit and validated.
  • Schema governance remains versioned and fail-closed.
  • Drift detection remains machine-readable and part of the federation gate.

docs.title: Intentionally deferred
docs.text:
• cryptographic signing infrastructure
  • trust-chain and key-management system
  • full explorer implementation
  • policy-plane expansion
  • advanced topography modeling
  • multi-environment deployment semantics
  • UI visualization systems

docs.title: Future change requirements
docs.text:
Any future semantic change in RepoGraph requires explicit schema-governance review. In particular:

  • new enum values require a version bump
  • removed enum values are breaking
  • boundary artifact field removal is breaking
  • projection rule removal is breaking
  • projection safety downgrades are breaking unless explicitly declared and reviewed

This checkpoint is a baseline marker, not a new architecture layer.

docs.text: Protocol Repo Owns CxRP CxRP cross-repo execution routing semantics and contract vocabulary RxP RxP normalized runtime invocation and result semantics Graph semantics RepoGraph shared repo identity, visibility, topology, and boundary schema Public projection PlatformManifest public-safe graph surface and boundary artifact publication

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• private-truth layer is not a browseable public repo; it is named here as a boundary participant.
  • WH (Warehouse) is utility-only — it packages context but does not govern or orchestrate.
  • PD (PlatformDeployment) feeds the control plane at the environment level.

docs.title: Execution Sequence
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Human
    participant Console as OperatorConsole
    participant OC as OperationsCenter
    participant SB as SwitchBoard
    participant ER as ExecutorRuntime
    participant Managed as Managed Project

    Human->>Console: request work
    Console->>OC: forward intent
    OC->>SB: route using canonical contract
    SB-->>OC: lane decision
    OC->>ER: runtime invocation request
    ER->>Managed: execute
    Managed-->>ER: artifacts
    ER-->>OC: runtime result

docs.title: Governance Enforcement
docs.text:
graph TD
    Change[Change or Generated Output]
    Policy[Invariant and Policy Rules]
    Custodian[Custodian]
    Review[Human Review]
    Release[Release or Publication]

    Change --> Policy
    Policy --> Custodian
    Custodian --> Review
    Review --> Release

docs.title: Public / Private Projection Flow
docs.text:
Shows how private truth becomes a public-safe projection and how enforcement consumes it.

graph TD
    PT[Private truth layer\nprivate graph truth]
    RG[RepoGraph\ngraph schema and semantics]
    RULES[Projection rules\nvisibility and redaction]
    PM[PlatformManifest\npublic-safe projection]
    BA[Boundary artifact\nfrozen export]
    CU[Custodian\nfail-closed verifier]
    OPS[OperationsCenter]
    OC[OperatorConsole]
    SB[SwitchBoard]

    PT -->|private truth export| RULES
    RG -->|schema and semantics| RULES
    RULES --> PM
    PM --> BA
    BA --> CU
    CU -->|verified context| OPS
    CU -->|verified context| OC
    CU -->|verified context| SB

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Private truth never flows directly to consumers — it passes through projection and redaction first.
  • PlatformManifest owns the public-safe projection surface; it does not own private truth.
  • Custodian fails closed when the boundary artifact is absent.
  • Consumers (OperationsCenter, OperatorConsole, SwitchBoard) receive verified public-safe context only.
  • Local manifests used at runtime are never published to the public surface.

docs.title: Manifest Projection
docs.text:
graph TD
    Private[Private truth layer\nprivate graph truth]
    Rules[Projection Rules]
    Public[PlatformManifest\npublic-safe projection]
    Local[Local Manifest\nruntime-only overlay]
    Custodian[Custodian]

    Private --> Rules
    Rules --> Public
    Local -. never published .-> Public
    Public --> Custodian

docs.title: Generated Documentation
docs.text:

  • schema indexes

The goal is to keep generated surfaces explainable, stable, and explicitly scoped.

  • Drift Detection

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture Charter
  • Semantic Federation
  • Custodian

docs.title: Architectural Invariants
docs.text:
Examples of important invariants:

  • protocol repos own canonical protocol semantics
  • manifest repos own topology and visibility truth
  • public outputs are safe projections, not independent drift copies
  • unknown visibility fails closed
  • orchestrators consume, but do not absorb, protocol or topology ownership

docs.title: Audit Policy
docs.text: Audit policy exists to decide what must fail, what may warn, and what requires human review.

docs.title: Typical outcomes
docs.text:
• PASS
  • WARN
  • FAIL
  • KNOWN_LEGACY
  • NOT_APPLICABLE

docs.title: Boundary Enforcement
docs.text: Boundary enforcement is the practice of stopping semantic responsibility drift.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• PlatformManifest must not absorb CxRP schemas
  • Custodian must not become ontology owner
  • OperationsCenter must not become topology owner
  • PlatformDeployment must not become orchestration owner

docs.title: Capability Governance
docs.text:
Capability governance answers:

  • which backends or runtimes are allowed
  • under what constraints
  • with what validation expectations
  • and under which policy gates

docs.title: Drift Detection
docs.text: Drift detection is the mechanism for finding when implementation, docs, or ownership semantics no longer match the intended architecture.

docs.title: Drift categories
docs.text:
• schema drift
  • terminology drift
  • repo responsibility drift
  • visibility drift
  • protocol mapping drift

docs.title: External Integrations
docs.text: External integrations are not automatically core ProtocolWarden repos. They become platform components only when an adapter, contract, and boundary story exists for them.

docs.title: Current examples
docs.text: Repo Role Notes Archon external integration candidate capability source firecrawl external integration candidate backend / crawl integration kodo external integration candidate backend or forked dependency PraisonAI external integration candidate backend or orchestration input Wan2GP external integration candidate capability source openclaw external integration candidate integration surface Zonos-API external integration candidate backend API neuro-game-sdk external integration candidate capability source

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Forks are not core architecture by default.
  • External repos are documented as dependencies, adapters, or capability sources.
  • A repo only becomes part of the platform when its contracts and boundaries are explicit.
  • SourceRegistry tracks source and fork lifecycle.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Public Repository Catalog
  • Profile README vs Pages Site
  • SourceRegistry

docs.title: Profile README vs Pages Site
docs.text:
ProtocolWarden exposes two different public-facing GitHub repositories:

Repo Purpose Where it appears ProtocolWarden/ProtocolWarden GitHub profile README repository github.com/ProtocolWarden profile front door ProtocolWarden/ProtocolWarden.github.io GitHub Pages documentation repository protocolwarden.github.io public knowledge surface

docs.title: Why both exist
docs.text:
• The profile README is the short front door for first-time visitors.
  • The Pages site is the full public architecture charter and documentation hub.
  • The two surfaces should work together, not duplicate each other.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• The profile README stays brief.
  • The Pages site carries the detailed architecture, repo catalog, ontology, topology, governance, and diagram content.
  • Private-truth repositories are described only where the boundary must be explained, not as browseable public catalog entries.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• Profile README repository
  • Public documentation site

docs.title: Public Repository Catalog
docs.text:
This page defines the public-surface catalog policy for the browseable repo index. It is a curation rule, not a privacy detector. Privacy detectors still own leakage and boundary enforcement.

The browseable repository catalog on this site is curated. It lists approved public repo pages and does not turn private-truth repos into first-class public catalog entries.

docs.title: Policy
docs.text:
• Architecture and charter pages may name private-truth layer when they describe the private-truth boundary.
  • The public repo catalog itself is limited to approved public pages.
  • This policy does not replace privacy detectors; it complements them.
  • The public site exposes current pages only.
  • External integrations and backend candidates are documented separately from the core repo catalog.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Overview
  • Architecture Charter
  • Ontology
  • Topology

  • External Integrations

docs.title: Runtime Governance
docs.text:
Runtime governance keeps runtime invocation and execution behavior bounded.

It should cover:

  • runtime selection
  • runtime binding policy
  • environment assumptions
  • invocation safety
  • artifact retention expectations

docs.title: Schema Evolution
docs.text: Schema evolution should be deliberate, versioned, and documented.

docs.title: Desired properties
docs.text:
• explicit ownership
  • compatibility notes
  • migration guidance
  • examples
  • deprecation policy

docs.title: Observability
docs.text: Observability explains how execution, artifacts, and drift remain visible over time.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Audit System
  • Representative Runs
  • Drift Analysis
  • Artifact Indexing
  • Execution Traces
  • Reporting

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Governance
  • Architecture Charter
  • Custodian

docs.title: Artifact Indexing
docs.text:
Artifact indexing is how evidence stays discoverable after execution is over.

Examples:

  • execution outputs
  • reports
  • traces
  • normalized references

docs.title: Audit System
docs.text:
The audit system exists to verify boundaries, preserve intent, and catch drift.

It should explain:

  • what is being checked
  • why it is being checked
  • who owns the semantics being enforced

docs.title: Drift Analysis
docs.text:
Drift analysis looks for divergence between:

  • docs and code
  • schemas and consumers
  • public and private projections
  • ownership doctrine and implementation

docs.title: Execution Traces
docs.text: Execution traces preserve how work moved through planning, routing, runtime, and artifact production layers.

docs.title: Reporting
docs.text:
Reporting should provide human-readable summaries of:

  • audits
  • drift findings
  • representative runs
  • compatibility state

docs.title: Representative Runs
docs.text: Representative runs are a way to show the platform behaving end to end under known scenarios. They provide stronger confidence than isolated unit semantics alone.

docs.title: Ontology
docs.text: Ontology is the shared vocabulary layer for the ProtocolWarden ecosystem.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Ontology vs Topology vs Topography
  • Graph Semantics
  • Entities
  • Vocabulary
  • Execution Objects
  • Governance Objects
  • Audit Objects
  • Glossary Index

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: The ontology section defines what the major concepts are, what they mean, and which repos own them.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture Charter
  • Topology
  • Glossary

docs.title: Audit Objects
docs.text:
Audit-related objects include:

  • audit plans
  • representative run reports
  • detector findings
  • verification summaries
  • policy exceptions
  • evidence references

The important distinction is that audit objects explain and enforce system behavior; they are not themselves runtime execution primitives.

docs.title: Entities
docs.text: This section tracks the main ecosystem entity types.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Project
  • Repository
  • ManagedProject
  • ManagedRepository
  • Manifest
  • Artifact
  • Run
  • Audit
  • Evidence
  • VisibilityPolicy
  • ProjectionRule
  • RuntimeBinding
  • CapabilitySet

For each entity, the important questions are:

  • what is it
  • who owns its semantics
  • where does it live
  • how does it relate to other entities

docs.title: Execution Objects
docs.text:
Core execution-related objects include:

  • TaskProposal
  • LaneDecision
  • ExecutionRequest
  • ExecutionResult
  • RuntimeBinding
  • CapabilitySet

docs.title: Ownership
docs.text:
• TaskProposal, LaneDecision, ExecutionRequest, ExecutionResult, RuntimeBinding, CapabilitySet: canonical wire semantics belong in CxRP
  • runtime invocation and return details belong in RxP
  • stricter internal orchestration models may exist in consumer repos, but they must map explicitly to canonical contracts

docs.title: Glossary Index
docs.text: See the Glossary for the canonical quick-reference surface. This ontology index exists to keep the larger concept system connected to the glossary.

docs.title: Governance Objects
docs.text:
Governance is expressed through objects such as:

  • invariant definitions
  • audit findings
  • policy descriptors
  • visibility rules
  • schema evolution guidance
  • drift reports

These objects should be explicit enough to support verification and review.

docs.title: Graph Semantics
docs.text: RepoGraph defines the shared graph language for ProtocolWarden.

docs.title: It owns
docs.text:
• repo identity semantics
  • visibility and disclosure semantics
  • edge and topology semantics
  • projection and redaction semantics
  • boundary artifact schema semantics
  • schema versioning and compatibility policy

docs.title: It does not own
docs.text:
• private graph truth
  • runtime orchestration
  • deployment control
  • UI projection logic
  • policy governance blobs

docs.title: Consumers
docs.text:
• PlatformManifest
  • Custodian
  • OperationsCenter
  • Profile README vs Pages Site

docs.title: Ontology vs Topology vs Topography
docs.text: This page keeps three similar words separate.

docs.title: Ontology
docs.text:
Ontology answers: what exists, and what does it mean?

Examples:

  • repo kinds
  • execution objects
  • governance objects
  • capability sets
  • contract terms

RepoGraph owns the canonical ontology vocabulary.

docs.title: Topology
docs.text:
Topology answers: how are the things connected?

Examples:

  • manifest consumption
  • projection publication
  • artifact reads and writes
  • orchestration dispatch
  • runtime invocation

RepoGraph owns the canonical topology vocabulary. Consumers import it; they do not redefine it.

docs.title: Topography
docs.text:
Topography answers: what does the operational terrain look like in a concrete environment?

Examples:

  • local paths
  • ports
  • compose files
  • env overlays
  • machine-specific runtime placement

PlatformDeployment owns concrete topography overlays. RepoGraph may define shared topography concepts, but it does not own deployment execution.

docs.title: Practical rule
docs.text:
• if you are naming things, you are probably in ontology
  • if you are wiring things together, you are probably in topology
  • if you are describing where things live, you are probably in topography

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture Charter
  • Ecosystem Overview
  • Repository Catalog

docs.title: Vocabulary
docs.text: Terminology must stay stable enough that boundaries remain understandable.

docs.title: Terms that need disciplined use
docs.text:
• ontology
  • topology
  • topography
  • projection
  • governance
  • runtime
  • orchestration
  • manifest
  • binding
  • capability

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: Do not casually rename canonical concepts across repos. If terminology changes, the docs should explicitly record the change and its rationale.

docs.title: Semantic Federation
docs.text: Semantic federation is the cross-repo governance gate that keeps the RepoGraph architecture durable after the migration and hardening passes.

docs.title: What it verifies
docs.text:
• RepoGraph importability
  • schema compatibility
  • projection profile safety
  • boundary artifact validity
  • ownership drift
  • duplicate graph vocabulary
  • legacy path regression

docs.title: Local execution
docs.text:
Use the Custodian governance gate against a workspace root and a materialized boundary artifact:

custodian-repograph-governance-gate \
  --repo-root /path/to/workspace \
  --boundary-artifact /path/to/boundary_disclosure_artifact.json \
  --json-out /tmp/repograph-governance.json \
  --summary-out /tmp/repograph-governance.md

docs.title: CI execution
docs.text: Custodian's semantic-federation.yml workflow materializes the boundary artifact into a temporary file, checks out the public repo set, and runs the same gate in GitHub Actions.

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
• a materialized RepoGraph boundary artifact file
  • a workspace root containing the public repos under test

docs.title: Failure interpretation
docs.text:
• missing boundary artifact: setup error, fail closed
  • malformed artifact: export or transport error, fail closed
  • ownership drift / duplicate vocabulary: RepoGraph or consumer regression
  • legacy path regression: a forbidden compatibility path reappeared

docs.title: Policy split
docs.text:
The public-surface catalog policy is separate from privacy detection:

  • custodian.policy.public_surface_catalog governs browseable repo pages
  • privacy detectors govern leakage, forbidden names, and boundary validity
  • architecture docs may still name private-truth layer when describing private truth, but the public catalog may not promote it to a first-class repo page

This workflow is operational glue only. It does not define graph semantics or boundary policy. It preserves the enforcement boundary by consuming the same artifact file as local operators and CI.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This section introduces the ProtocolWarden ecosystem at a human level before you dive into contracts, architecture, or topology.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• Ecosystem
  • Ecosystem Role Matrix
  • Design Philosophy
  • Public vs Private
  • Architecture Principles
  • Operational Model
  • Ecosystem Map

docs.title: Core questions
docs.text:
• What exists?
  • Why does it exist?
  • How does it connect?
  • What is public?
  • What stays private?

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Repository Catalog
  • Ontology: Ontology vs Topology vs Topography
  • Profile README vs Pages Site
  • Simple Platform Model

docs.title: Clear Ownership
docs.text: Every important concept should have a clear owner repo.

docs.title: Boundary Discipline
docs.text: Systems may consume each other, but they should not quietly absorb each other's semantic responsibilities.

docs.title: Explicit Mapping
docs.text: When internal models differ from wire contracts, mapping should be explicit and tested.

docs.title: Fail Closed
docs.text: Unknown visibility, projection, or policy behavior should fail closed.

docs.title: Human-Comprehension Layer
docs.text: This documentation repository exists to make the platform understandable without inspecting every codebase at once.

docs.title: Design Philosophy
docs.text: The ecosystem follows a few consistent design rules.

docs.title: Contract-First Architecture
docs.text: The system prefers explicit, versioned contracts over implicit integration behavior.

docs.title: Adapters Before Forks
docs.text: Integrations should prefer adapters and boundary mappers before pulling foreign semantics into platform core.

docs.title: Public Projection
docs.text: Public documentation and manifests must be projection-driven, not manually forked from private truth.

docs.title: Governance-Driven Evolution
docs.text: Architectural changes should come with explicit boundary reasoning, tests, and invariant checks.

docs.title: Audit-Driven Reliability
docs.text: Reliability is strengthened through audits, representative verification passes, and drift detection instead of relying only on intent.

docs.title: Ecosystem Role Matrix
docs.text:
This matrix is the fast answer to “what owns what?” for the public ecosystem.

Layer Purpose Main repos Profile Front Door short public orientation ProtocolWarden/ProtocolWarden Public Knowledge Surface canonical documentation and public projection ProtocolWarden.github.io Semantic Graph Layer shared graph language and schema governance RepoGraph, PlatformManifest Contract Protocol Layer execution and runtime contracts CxRP, RxP Control Plane Layer planning and orchestration consumer OperationsCenter Routing Policy Layer lane and backend selection SwitchBoard Operator Entry Layer operator workspace and command surface OperatorConsole Runtime Execution Layer invocation mechanics and adapters ExecutorRuntime, backend consumers Governance / Audit Layer boundary, drift, and catalog enforcement Custodian Deployment / Local Runtime Layer environment assembly and convenience wrappers PlatformDeployment Source Lifecycle Layer source and fork inventory SourceRegistry Context Staging Layer LLM-ready packaging and staging Warehouse External Backend Layer adapter candidates and forked dependencies Archon, firecrawl, kodo, PraisonAI, Wan2GP, openclaw, Zonos-API, neuro-game-sdk

docs.title: Boundary reminder
docs.text:
• private-truth repositories are documentation and boundary sources, not browseable public catalog entries.
  • The public site explains it where the boundary needs to be understood.

docs.title: Ecosystem
docs.text: ProtocolWarden is a multi-repository ecosystem organized around explicit contracts, visible ownership boundaries, and public/private documentation projection.

docs.title: Core Mission
docs.text:
The ecosystem is designed to coordinate:

  • planning
  • routing
  • execution
  • runtime invocation
  • artifact production
  • policy enforcement
  • topology management
  • public-safe documentation

without collapsing all semantics into one repository.

docs.title: Public Surface
docs.text:
• ProtocolWarden/ProtocolWarden
  • ProtocolWarden.github.io

docs.title: Semantic Core
docs.text:
• RepoGraph
  • PlatformManifest
  • CxRP
  • RxP

docs.title: Control Plane
docs.text:
• OperatorConsole
  • OperationsCenter
  • SwitchBoard
  • ExecutorRuntime

docs.title: Platform Inventory and Governance
docs.text:
• Custodian
  • SourceRegistry
  • PlatformDeployment
  • Warehouse

docs.title: Private Truth Boundary
docs.text: • private-truth repositories

docs.title: Managed Projects and Capability Providers
docs.text:
• managed private projects
  • backend and tool integrations
  • external integrations and forked dependencies

docs.title: Why the Repo Split Exists
docs.text:
The split is deliberate:

  • protocols remain canonical in protocol repos
  • orchestration remains canonical in orchestration repos
  • runtime mechanics remain canonical in runtime repos
  • topology and visibility remain canonical in manifest repos
  • public documentation remains canonical in this repo
  • managed projects remain consumers of the published ecosystem, not owners of it
  • RepoGraph remains the shared semantic substrate

That separation reduces schema duplication, ownership drift, and god-repo behavior.

docs.title: Operational Model
docs.text:
At a high level:

  1. work intent enters through human or system-facing surfaces
  2. OperationsCenter plans and governs work
  3. SwitchBoard selects a lane and backend
  4. ExecutorRuntime or backend adapters perform runtime invocation
  5. artifacts, evidence, and traces are retained
  6. Custodian and governance surfaces validate policy and drift boundaries
  7. public-safe outputs are documented or projected separately

This model keeps execution, governance, topology, and documentation distinct.

docs.title: Public vs Private
docs.text: ProtocolWarden explicitly separates public-facing knowledge from private operational truth.

docs.title: Public
docs.text:
Public-safe material may include:

  • public repo identities
  • public protocol descriptions
  • public-safe topology
  • stable public documentation
  • sanitized diagrams
  • public examples

docs.title: Private
docs.text:
Private material may include:

  • internal topology
  • private managed project details
  • internal paths and endpoints
  • private runtime or deployment assumptions
  • restricted relationship edges

docs.title: Core Rule
docs.text:
Public knowledge should be a projection of private truth when those surfaces are related. This avoids documentation drift and privacy leakage.

The projection boundary is not a manual copy step. RepoGraph defines the projection rules, the private-truth layer generates boundary artifacts from private truth, and Custodian verifies public surfaces against those artifacts.

The public site is current-only. It does not catalog private-truth repos as first-class browseable pages.

The short public front door lives in the separate ProtocolWarden profile README repository. The full public knowledge surface lives here.

docs.title: Protocols
docs.text: This section is the public protocol and specification hub.

docs.title: Main protocol surfaces
docs.text:
• CxRP — execution and routing semantics
  • RxP — runtime invocation semantics
  • PlatformManifest — topology and visibility semantics

Related surfaces:

  • Execution Contracts
  • Runtime Contracts
  • PlatformManifest
  • CxRP
  • RxP

docs.title: CxRP
docs.text: CxRP owns canonical cross-repo execution and routing contract semantics.

docs.title: Main objects
docs.text:
• TaskProposal
  • LaneDecision
  • ExecutionRequest
  • ExecutionResult
  • RuntimeBinding
  • CapabilitySet

docs.title: What CxRP Is Not
docs.text:
• not topology ownership
  • not deployment ownership
  • not runtime-hosting ownership
  • not orchestration implementation

docs.title: Execution Contracts
docs.text:
Execution contracts describe the planning, routing, and execution object model used across repo boundaries.

The canonical public contract owner is CxRP.

docs.title: PlatformManifest
docs.text: PlatformManifest publishes the public graph instance for the ecosystem using shared RepoGraph semantics.

docs.title: It answers
docs.text:
• what exists in the public platform view
  • how public entities relate
  • which public-safe projection is published

docs.title: It does not answer
docs.text:
• execution contract semantics
  • runtime invocation semantics
  • orchestration implementation
  • canonical ontology/topology/projection semantics

docs.title: Runtime Contracts
docs.text:
Runtime contracts describe invocation payloads, runtime outcomes, and runtime artifact references.

The canonical public contract owner is RxP.

docs.title: RxP
docs.text: RxP owns runtime invocation and return semantics.

docs.title: Main objects
docs.text:
• RuntimeInvocation
  • RuntimeResult
  • transport and runtime metadata

docs.title: Why it exists
docs.text: It separates runtime mechanics from orchestration semantics and keeps runtime boundaries explicit.

docs.title: Repository Catalog
docs.text: This catalog explains what each major repo does and, more importantly, what it does not do.

docs.title: Core platform repos
docs.text: Repo Category Role RepoGraph semantic_core shared graph language, schema governance, projection semantics, boundary artifact semantics PlatformManifest topology_projection public-safe graph publisher and projection surface CxRP contract_protocol contract execution routing semantics RxP contract_protocol runtime execution semantics OperationsCenter control_plane orchestration consumer and execution coordinator SwitchBoard routing_policy lane and backend selection OperatorConsole operator_entrypoint operator-facing control surface ExecutorRuntime runtime_execution runtime invocation mechanics and adapters Custodian governance_audit boundary, drift, and semantic federation verifier PlatformDeployment deployment_runtime runtime glue and local/CI ergonomics SourceRegistry source_lifecycle source and fork inventory Warehouse context_staging LLM-ready packaging and staging

docs.title: Adjacent surfaces
docs.text: Repo Category Notes External Integrations integration_surface forks, adapters, and backend candidates documented separately

docs.title: Boundary note
docs.text: Private-truth repos are not normal browsable repo pages. The architecture charter and boundary workflow docs explain the boundary without promoting those repos into first-class catalog entries.

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Privacy, hygiene, and drift detection enforcement.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• detector runtime
  • audit and hygiene enforcement surface
  • RepoGraph boundary artifact consumer
  • policy violation reporting layer

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• topology owner
  • orchestration owner
  • runtime invoker
  • private-name policy owner

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Canonical cross-repo execution and routing contracts.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• wire contract owner
  • schema owner
  • contract vocabulary owner

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• orchestration implementation
  • topology system
  • runtime host

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Runtime execution mechanics for bounded invocation.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• runtime invocation implementation
  • backend runtime driver

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• orchestration planner
  • topology owner

docs.title: External Integrations
docs.text: This page groups repos that are relevant to the ecosystem but are not core ProtocolWarden semantic authority.

docs.title: Categories
docs.text:
• Forked dependency
  • Adapter candidate
  • Backend candidate
  • External capability source

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Archon
  • firecrawl
  • kodo
  • PraisonAI
  • Wan2GP

  • Zonos-API
  • neuro-game-sdk

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: These repos are not equal to core platform repos. They only become platform components when the contract and boundary story is explicit.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• External Integrations policy
  • SourceRegistry

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Orchestration and runtime coordination for the ProtocolWarden control plane.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• orchestration consumer
  • runtime dispatch owner
  • execution coordinator
  • consumer of RepoGraph-backed manifests and boundary artifacts
  • evidence and observability integration surface

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• canonical protocol owner
  • canonical graph vocabulary owner
  • canonical semantic vocabulary owner
  • deployment owner
  • runtime-hosting layer
  • graph semantics authority
  • projection semantics owner
  • topology language owner

docs.title: Depends On
docs.text:
• CxRP
  • RxP
  • SwitchBoard
  • ExecutorRuntime
  • PlatformManifest
  • private-truth layer
  • RepoGraph-backed public and private inputs

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Human-facing entrypoint into the control plane.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• operator interaction surface
  • top-level entrypoint for human-driven work

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• canonical protocol owner
  • runtime system
  • topology authority

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Runtime glue and deployment/topography overlay support for the ProtocolWarden ecosystem.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• local deployment and hosting layer
  • machine-specific topography and runtime placement surface
  • environment assembly support
  • convenience wrapper host for boundary-artifact-driven Custodian runs

docs.title: This Repo Is Not

  • graph language owner
  • ontology owner
  • projection owner

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture Charter
  • Semantic Federation

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Public graph instance and projection publisher built on RepoGraph.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• public platform inventory
  • public-safe graph publisher
  • RepoGraph consumer

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• execution contract owner
  • runtime contract owner
  • orchestration implementation
  • canonical ontology/topology/projection owner

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Canonical shared graph-semantics library for the ProtocolWarden platform.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• canonical graph language authority
  • schema governance owner
  • projection and boundary semantics owner
  • shared vocabulary for repo identity, visibility, disclosure, and topology

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• private graph truth source
  • runtime orchestrator
  • deployment manager
  • UI projection engine
  • policy blob

docs.title: Depends On
docs.text:
• none for canonical semantics
  • Custodian for enforcement
  • PlatformManifest for public projection publication
  • private-truth layer for private truth export

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Graph Semantics
  • RepoGraph Boundary
  • RepoGraph README

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Canonical runtime invocation and runtime result contracts.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• runtime wire contract owner
  • runtime payload schema owner

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• planning layer
  • topology owner
  • deployment system

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Registry of source and repository metadata relevant to the ecosystem.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• source and repo inventory support
  • reference and lookup surface

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• execution orchestrator
  • public docs site
  • runtime invocation contract owner

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Lane and backend selection.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• routing and recommendation logic
  • lane-selection policy surface

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• orchestration implementation
  • runtime execution layer
  • topology owner

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Code-context packaging and staging for LLM-oriented workflows.

docs.title: This Repo Is
docs.text:
• context kit and crate builder
  • staging surface for curated code bundles
  • pallet materialization support
  • debugging and operator-support utility

docs.title: This Repo Is Not
docs.text:
• registry owner
  • orchestration owner
  • topology owner
  • runtime execution surface
  • platform authority boundary
  • governance authority

docs.title: Immediate
docs.text:
• establish MkDocs site structure
  • create canonical repo pages
  • add ecosystem diagrams
  • normalize terminology

docs.title: Next
docs.text:
• extract canonical docs from source repos
  • add generated schema and vocabulary pages
  • add compatibility matrices
  • wire GitHub Pages or CI publish automation
  • add repo-specific diagrams and source-linked cross references

docs.title: Backlogged Slices
docs.text: • Slice 01 - Canonical Docs Consolidation

docs.title: Later
docs.text:
• automate inventory extraction
  • publish generated topology and audit reports
  • add versioned protocol documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Replace first-pass ecosystem prose with extracted, normalized, and public-safe canonical material from the live ProtocolWarden repositories.

This slice is the transition from:

  • initial site scaffold
  • first-pass repo summaries
  • broad architecture framing

to:

  • repo-derived canonical docs
  • protocol-derived object pages
  • generated public-safe indexes
  • publishable public ecosystem documentation

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
1. replace broad first-pass prose with canonical material extracted from source repos
  2. add generated schema and enum documentation pages
  3. wire GitHub Pages or equivalent CI publication workflow
  4. add repo-specific diagrams and source-linked cross references

docs.title: Inputs
docs.text:
Expected source repos for extraction:

  • OperationsCenter
  • CxRP
  • RxP
  • PlatformManifest
  • private-truth layer
  • Custodian
  • ExecutorRuntime
  • SwitchBoard
  • managed private projects
  • SourceRegistry
  • PlatformDeployment
  • Warehouse

Expected source materials:

  • README files
  • architecture docs
  • verification summaries
  • protocol docs
  • schema comments
  • topology docs
  • governance docs

docs.title: D1. Canonical repo pages
docs.text:
Replace the current high-level repo pages with repo-derived material for:

  • OperationsCenter
  • CxRP
  • RxP
  • PlatformManifest
  • private-truth layer
  • Custodian
  • ExecutorRuntime
  • SwitchBoard
  • managed private projects

Each page should include:

  • mission
  • what this repo is
  • what this repo is not
  • ownership boundaries
  • integration points
  • public-safe diagrams

docs.title: D2. Generated public-safe contract surface
docs.text:
Add generated or semi-generated pages for:

  • schema indexes
  • enum indexes
  • vocabulary indexes
  • compatibility tables

These belong under generated/ and should be derived from canonical owners, not hand-maintained duplicates.

docs.title: D3. Publication workflow
docs.text:
Add CI or GitHub Pages publication wiring so the site can be built and published from the repository directly.

Minimum deliverables:

  • docs build job
  • publish job
  • build failure on broken navigation or markdown references

docs.title: D4. Diagram enrichment
docs.text:
Add public-safe diagrams for:

  • control-plane boundaries
  • protocol boundaries
  • manifest projection flow
  • execution sequence
  • repo constellation

docs.title: D5. Cross-linking
docs.text:
Add source-aware cross-links between:

  • overview pages
  • repo pages
  • protocol pages
  • glossary and ontology pages

docs.title: Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
This slice is complete when:

  • repo pages use extracted or normalized canonical material instead of only first-pass summaries
  • generated contract and vocabulary pages exist
  • the site builds in CI
  • the site has a publish path
  • diagrams cover the main ecosystem boundary surfaces
  • cross-links make the site navigable without repo-by-repo reverse engineering

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This slice does not:

  • publish private topology
  • duplicate private operational docs
  • redefine protocol ownership
  • move runtime or orchestration logic into the docs repo
  • make the docs repo a runtime dependency

docs.title: Follow-on Slices
docs.text:
Likely follow-on work after this slice:

  • automated extraction tooling
  • versioned protocol docs
  • generated topology maps
  • generated audit summaries

docs.title: Topology
docs.text: Topology explains how the ecosystem is connected.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Ecosystem Graph
  • RepoGraph Boundary
  • Semantic Boundary Workflow
  • Repo Dependencies
  • Execution Routing
  • Service Relationships
  • Artifact Flow
  • Control Plane

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Architecture Charter
  • Ontology
  • Repository Catalog

docs.title: Artifact Flow
docs.text:
Artifacts connect runtime work to governance and observability surfaces.

They should be thought of as:

  • produced outputs
  • retained evidence
  • references for audit and traceability
  • public-safe metadata candidates after projection

docs.title: Control Plane
docs.text: The control plane is the part of the ecosystem that plans, routes, and governs.

docs.title: Control-plane repos
docs.text:
• OperatorConsole
  • OperationsCenter
  • SwitchBoard
  • CxRP
  • RxP
  • ExecutorRuntime

docs.title: Non-control-plane repos
docs.text:
• PlatformManifest
  • private-truth layer
  • Custodian
  • PlatformDeployment
  • Warehouse
  • managed project repos

Those support or constrain the control plane, but they do not replace it.

docs.title: Ecosystem Graph
docs.text:
graph TD
    FRONT[ProtocolWarden/ProtocolWarden]
    OC[OperatorConsole]
    OPS[OperationsCenter]
    SB[SwitchBoard]
    CX[CxRP]
    RX[RxP]
    ER[ExecutorRuntime]
    RG[RepoGraph]
    PM[PlatformManifest]
    PT[Private truth layer]
    CU[Custodian]
    WS[PlatformDeployment]
    WH[Warehouse]

    FRONT --> DOCS
    DOCS --> PM

    DOCS --> RX
    OC --> OPS
    OPS --> SB
    OPS --> CX
    OPS --> RX
    OPS --> ER
    OPS --> PM
    OPS --> PT
    OPS --> SR
    OPS --> CU
    WS --> OPS
    WH --> OPS

docs.title: Execution Routing
docs.text: Execution routing is the handoff from planning intent to lane and backend selection.

docs.title: Main path
docs.text:
• OperationsCenter produces or maps a proposal
  • SwitchBoard returns a routing decision
  • OperationsCenter binds runtime and capability context
  • ExecutorRuntime or adapters perform invocation

docs.title: Repo Dependencies
docs.text:
Dependency direction should follow ownership boundaries:

  • docs depend on public-safe knowledge surfaces
  • governance depends on protocol and topology surfaces
  • orchestration depends on protocols, runtime, and manifest layers
  • runtime layers should not quietly own orchestration semantics

docs.title: RepoGraph Boundary
docs.text: RepoGraph defines the language. It does not own the private truth that is expressed in that language.

docs.title: Boundary split
docs.text: Repo Responsibility RepoGraph graph vocabulary, schema governance, projection semantics, boundary artifact shape PlatformManifest public-safe projection publisher private-truth layer private truth source and boundary artifact generation Custodian fail-closed verification of boundary artifacts OperationsCenter consumes graph context, does not own semantics

docs.title: Boundary rule
docs.text: Automation may materialize the boundary artifact. Enforcement only consumes it. Custodian must fail closed when the required boundary artifact is absent.

docs.title: Semantic Boundary Workflow
docs.text:
This page describes the current enforcement path from private truth to public verification.

flowchart LR
    RG[RepoGraph] -->|shared semantics| PM[PlatformManifest]
    PRM[private-truth layer] -->|private truth + export| BA[Boundary artifact]
    PM -->|public-safe projection| BA
    BA --> CU[Custodian]
    CU --> OC[OperationsCenter]
    CU --> OPS[OperatorConsole]
    CU --> SB[SwitchBoard]

docs.title: Core flow
docs.text:
1. RepoGraph defines the shared graph language.
  2. private-truth layer owns private truth and exports the boundary artifact.
  3. PlatformManifest publishes the public-safe projection surface.
  4. Custodian validates the boundary artifact before scanning public repos.
  5. OperationsCenter, OperatorConsole, and SwitchBoard consume verified public-safe context without owning graph semantics.

docs.title: Important rule
docs.text: Custodian fails closed when REPOGRAPH_BOUNDARY_ARTIFACT_FILE is missing. Automation belongs before Custodian runs, not inside Custodian's semantic core.

docs.title: Service Relationships
docs.text:
This ecosystem contains code repositories, protocol repositories, manifest repositories, and managed project repositories. The relationships are not all service-to-service in the narrow sense.

Important relationship categories:

  • owns canonical schema
  • consumes topology
  • validates policy
  • invokes runtime
  • produces artifacts
  • hosts local deployment
